name: language-selector
title: Language selector
description: Switches a site between languages with each language named in its OWN language (its endonym) and announced in the right language via per-item lang/hreflang. Two presentations from one contract: an inline toggle for two-language (bilingual) sites and the Commons native Select dropdown for three or more. A universal globe plus an accessible name, non-color active state, RTL-safe endonyms, 44px targets, and forced-colors borders. Fires onLanguageChange(code) and leaves routing to the consumer.
useWhen: Letting people read a government site in their own language
A bilingual site needs a compact English | Español toggle
A multilingual site needs a language dropdown placed consistently in the header/utility nav
avoidWhen: Choosing a form value that happens to be a language (use a Select/combo-box with translated labels for that context)
Only one language is offered (render nothing)
You need automatic machine translation UI rather than switching between human-translated versions

keyboard: Toggle: Tab moves to each language link/button; Shift+Tab moves back; Enter (link) or Enter/Space (button) selects
Dropdown: the native Select's own keyboard model — Tab to focus, Arrow keys and type-ahead to move through languages, Enter/Escape to commit or dismiss
A disabled control is removed from the tab order
accessibility.targetSize: 44px project default (each toggle item is min-h-11; the dropdown select meets the same minimum)
notes: Each language is labeled by its endonym (its name in its own language), never translated into the current page language, so speakers recognize their language.
Every option/link carries lang (and, for links, hreflang) set to its own BCP-47 tag, so assistive tech pronounces the endonym in the correct language.
The active language is signaled by font weight plus aria-current, not color alone, so it survives forced-colors mode; the toggle and dropdown keep visible borders in every state.
The control always has an accessible name (default 'Select language') alongside a universal globe icon, because a text label alone may be in a language the reader does not know.
Endonyms render with dir=auto so a right-to-left name (e.g. العربية) lays out correctly even on a left-to-right page, and the component uses logical properties throughout so it is safe on RTL pages.
Selecting a language fires onLanguageChange(code); the component does not navigate, leaving routing to the consumer (the link variant carries a real href per language).
